icd_category: F06.1 — Catatonic disorder due to known physiological condition
one_liner: A 26-year-old man with schizophrenia presents with sudden-onset mutism, waxy flexibility, staring, and refusal to eat or drink for 48 hours.

stage2.hpi_extension: The patient was brought to the ED by his group home staff who report that 48 hours ago he suddenly stopped speaking, eating, and drinking. He was previously at his baseline, compliant with medications, and attending his day program. He is now found in a fixed posture — standing rigidly with his arm raised above his head. When staff physically reposition his limbs, he maintains the new position for prolonged periods (minutes to hours). He intermittently stares blankly and does not respond to verbal commands or painful stimuli. Staff report one episode where he suddenly became agitated, screamed, and threw a chair before returning to immobility. He has not urinated in approximately 18 hours. His last known medications were taken 3 days ago. No recent medication changes, no new medications added. No recent illness, trauma, or substance use. Temperature at the group home was noted to be 100.4°F.
stage2.pmh: Schizophrenia, paranoid type (diagnosed age 19). One prior catatonic episode 4 years ago (resolved with lorazepam). Type 2 diabetes mellitus. Obesity.
stage2.medications: Clozapine 300 mg at bedtime, metformin 1000 mg BID, lorazepam 1 mg PRN (not used recently)
stage2.family_history: Mother with schizophrenia. Father with alcohol use disorder. Maternal uncle with bipolar disorder and history of this condition.
stage2.social_history: Lives in a supervised group home. Receives SSI disability. Never employed. Single, no children. Attends day program 3 days per week. Denies tobacco, alcohol, or illicit drug use. Graduated high school with support services. Legal guardian is his mother.
stage2.physical_exam: General: **Obese young man standing rigidly with right arm extended above head, eyes open and staring, does not acknowledge examiner**; **Diaphoretic**. HEENT: **Pupils equal and reactive, eyes open with minimal blinking, dry mucous membranes**. Neck: **Mild rigidity, no meningismus**. Cardiovascular: **Tachycardic, regular rhythm, no murmurs**. Lungs: Clear to auscultation bilaterally, mildly tachypneic. Abdomen: **Soft, non-distended, unable to assess tenderness due to uncooperativeness**. Extremities: No edema. Skin: **diaphoretic, no rash**; **Neurological: Waxy flexibility (catalepsy) — limbs remain in examiner-placed positions for sustained periods**; **Negativism — active resistance to passive movement**; **Deep tendon reflexes 2+ symmetric**; **Babinski negative bilaterally**; **Mental Status Exam: Appearance — disheveled, diaphoretic**; **Behavior — immobile, mute (stuporous this condition) with one witnessed episode of sudden purposeless agitation**; **Speech — mute, no verbal output**; **Mood — unable to assess**; **Affect — flat, absent emotional expression**; **Thought process — unable to assess**; **Thought content — unable to assess**; **Perception — unable to assess, though intermittently appears to visually track movement**; **Cognition — unable to formally assess, not following commands**; **Bush-Francis this condition Rating Scale: score 28 (severe this condition) — positive items include immobility, mutism, staring, catalepsy (waxy flexibility), negativism, posturing, autonomic abnormality**.

lab_results.clinical_significance: Leukocytosis — may reflect stress response, dehydration, or emerging NMS; not specific

lab_results.result: Na 149, K 3.4, Cl 112, CO2 20, BUN 38, Cr 1.6, Glucose 245, Ca 9.0, AST 45, ALT 38, Alk Phos 72, Albumin 3.6
lab_results.clinical_significance: Hypernatremia and elevated BUN/Cr ratio indicating dehydration; hyperglycemia from diabetes and stress response; mild transaminase elevation
Elevated CPK from sustained posturing and immobility; needs monitoring as marked elevation would suggest NMS

lab_results.result: Dark amber, specific gravity 1.035, ketones moderate, no myoglobin
lab_results.clinical_significance: Concentrated urine with ketones from dehydration and poor oral intake; no myoglobinuria (reassuring against rhabdomyolysis)
Normal thyroid function, ruling out thyroid-related psychiatric presentation
Therapeutic range 350-600 ng/mL; sub-therapeutic level suggests possible non-adherence contributing to catatonic episode
Low serum iron is associated with this condition and NMS; may serve as a biomarker for neuroleptic sensitivity
No evidence of bacteremia; fever likely related to this condition itself (autonomic dysfunction)
Mildly elevated from dehydration and muscular exertion of sustained posturing

imaging_results.result: No acute intracranial abnormality. No mass, hemorrhage, or hydrocephalus.
Clear lung fields, no infiltrate. Normal cardiac silhouette.

key_tests: Bush-Francis Catatonia Rating Scale — score 28 confirming severe catatonia (catalepsy, mutism, staring, negativism, posturing, autonomic instability)
Lorazepam challenge (1-2 mg IV) — partial response within 10 minutes with decreased rigidity and brief verbalization, confirming catatonia
CPK — elevated at 680, requires serial monitoring to distinguish from NMS
Clozapine level — sub-therapeutic at 180 ng/mL suggesting non-adherence as potential trigger
acceptable_tests: CMP — identifying dehydration, AKI, and metabolic derangements
CBC — assessing for leukocytosis (infection vs. stress response)
low_yield_tests: Lumbar puncture (no meningeal signs, consider only if anti-NMDA receptor encephalitis suspected)
Toxicology panel beyond UDS (no exposure history)

stage5.final_clue: The patient presents with classic catatonic features: mutism, catalepsy (waxy flexibility), staring, negativism, posturing, and autonomic instability (fever, tachycardia, diaphoresis, hypertension), scoring 28 on the Bush-Francis this condition Rating Scale. The positive lorazepam challenge (partial improvement with IV benzodiazepine). The sub-therapeutic clozapine level suggests non-adherence as the likely precipitant in a patient with a prior catatonic episode. CPK is elevated but not in the range typical of NMS, and there is no lead-pipe rigidity.

debrief.classic_presentation: A patient with an underlying psychiatric or medical condition who develops sudden-onset motor abnormalities including immobility/stupor, mutism, staring, catalepsy (waxy flexibility), posturing, negativism, and/or stereotypies. Autonomic instability (fever, tachycardia, blood pressure lability, diaphoresis) indicates malignant catatonia, which is a medical emergency. The condition can also present as excited catatonia with purposeless agitation.
debrief.attending_mode_notes: The critical clinical distinction is between catatonia and neuroleptic malignant syndrome (NMS), as they share overlapping features (rigidity, fever, elevated CPK, autonomic instability) but require opposite treatments. NMS features lead-pipe rigidity (not waxy flexibility), is triggered by dopamine antagonists (especially typical antipsychotics or rapid dose increases), and requires dopamine agonists (bromocriptine) and dantrolene. Catatonia features waxy flexibility, can be triggered by antipsychotic withdrawal (as possibly occurred here with sub-therapeutic clozapine), and responds to benzodiazepines and ECT. Giving antipsychotics to catatonic patients can worsen the condition or precipitate NMS. The lorazepam challenge is both diagnostic and therapeutic — administer 1-2 mg IV lorazepam and observe for improvement within 5-10 minutes. Low serum iron (<45 mcg/dL) is an emerging biomarker associated with both catatonia and NMS susceptibility. In treatment-refractory catatonia or malignant catatonia with autonomic instability, ECT is the definitive treatment and should not be delayed. Anti-NMDA receptor encephalitis should be considered in the differential, particularly in younger patients.
debrief.pathophysiology: Catatonia is a neuropsychiatric syndrome, not a single disease. The leading neurochemical hypothesis involves top-down cortical GABA-A hypoactivity in orbital frontal and motor cortex, combined with excessive glutamatergic activity. This explains the therapeutic response to benzodiazepines (GABA-A agonists). Dopamine hypoactivity, particularly in the basal ganglia-thalamocortical circuits, contributes to motor abnormalities. The 'fear paralysis' model proposes that catatonia represents an evolutionarily conserved tonic immobility response (akin to animal freezing behavior) triggered by overwhelming internal distress. Autonomic instability in malignant catatonia results from hypothalamic dysregulation. Low serum iron may impair D2 receptor function, as iron is a cofactor for dopamine receptor sensitivity.
debrief.treatment_overview: First-line: IV lorazepam 1-2 mg, repeat every 3-4 hours as needed; if responsive, transition to standing high-dose lorazepam (up to 16-24 mg/day in divided doses). Response rate to benzodiazepines is ~70-80%. Second-line (or first-line for malignant catatonia): ECT — bilateral electrode placement, typically 6-10 sessions. ECT response rate exceeds 80%. AVOID antipsychotics during the acute catatonic episode, as they can worsen catatonia or precipitate NMS. Once catatonia resolves, cautiously resume/optimize the underlying psychiatric treatment (in this case, ensure clozapine adherence). Supportive care: aggressive IV hydration, DVT prophylaxis (immobilized patient), nutritional support, bladder catheterization if urinary retention, and continuous monitoring of CPK, electrolytes, and renal function.
high_yield_pearls: The lorazepam challenge is both diagnostic and therapeutic: 1-2 mg IV lorazepam with improvement within 5-10 minutes strongly supports catatonia over NMS or primary psychotic process.

Catatonia vs NMS: catatonia features waxy flexibility and responds to benzodiazepines; NMS features lead-pipe rigidity and requires dopamine agonists/dantrolene. They share fever, CPK elevation, and autonomic instability.
AVOID antipsychotics in acute catatonia — they can worsen the condition or trigger NMS. This is a critical management pearl.
ECT is the most effective treatment for catatonia (>80% response rate) and should be considered early in malignant catatonia or benzodiazepine-refractory cases.

Catatonia is not exclusive to schizophrenia — it is more commonly associated with mood disorders (especially bipolar), medical conditions (autoimmune encephalitis, metabolic), and substance-related causes.
Low serum iron (<45 mcg/dL) is an emerging biomarker associated with susceptibility to both catatonia and NMS.
sources: DSM-5-TR: Diagnostic and Statistical Manual of Mental Disorders, 5th Edition, Text Revision (2022)
Bush G et al. Catatonia I: Rating scale and standardized examination. Acta Psychiatr Scand, 1996
Rasmussen SA et al. Electroconvulsive therapy for catatonia: a review. J ECT, 2016
Sienaert P et al. A clinical review of the treatment of catatonia. Front Psychiatry, 2014
Fink M, Taylor MA. Catatonia: A Clinician's Guide to Diagnosis and Treatment. Cambridge University Press, 2003

after_stage2_more_likely: Catatonia — classic features (catalepsy/waxy flexibility, mutism, staring, negativism, posturing) with positive Bush-Francis score, prior catatonic episode, family history
NMS — fever, tachycardia, diaphoresis, rigidity, and autonomic instability overlap with catatonia
after_stage2_less_likely: Serotonin syndrome — no serotonergic medications, waxy flexibility rather than clonus/myoclonus
Non-convulsive status epilepticus — no seizure history, presentation is classic for catatonia
after_labs_top3: Catatonia — classic clinical features, positive lorazepam challenge, sub-therapeutic clozapine (withdrawal as precipitant), moderately elevated CPK, low serum iron
Neuroleptic malignant syndrome — cannot be fully excluded given fever, CPK elevation, and autonomic instability, but waxy flexibility (not lead-pipe rigidity) and lorazepam response favor catatonia
Anti-NMDA receptor encephalitis — should be considered in young patients with catatonia, though typical course includes prodromal psychiatric symptoms and seizures; antibody testing could be sent
